message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (for a code change the named test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if reverted; for a doc/decision item, judge whether the documented claim is now accurate/honest). Repo /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix H-008 in .github/workflows/ci.yml
What: top-level permissions: contents: read (least privilege for GITHUB_TOKEN).
Test(s): test_ci_has_least_privilege_permissions
Read the actual changed file(s); read the test; check no regression. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN. Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
name: ci

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
  core:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
          pip install --no-deps -e .
      - name: Lint (ruff)
        run: ruff check src tests
      - name: Format check (ruff)
        run: ruff format --check src tests
      - name: Type-check (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Test (pytest)
        run: pytest -q

  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
  # e2e) run for real.
  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
          POSTGRES_DB: assay
        ports: ["5432:5432"]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
      qdrant:
        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
        ports: ["6333:6333"]
    env:
      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --no-deps -e .
      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
          done
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
          done
          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing

  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install pip-audit
        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
        run: |
          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
          # stay in sync too.
          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
          IGNORES=()
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
            done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
          IGNORES=()
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
            done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
        with:
          name: sbom-cyclonedx
          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
          if-no-files-found: error
      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --no-deps -e .
          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
          pip-licenses --format=json --with-system > licenses.json
          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
      - name: Upload license report
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
        with:
          name: license-report
          path: licenses.json
          if-no-files-found: warn

  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
  all-checks:
    if: always()
    needs: [core, integration, audit]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
        run: |
          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
          echo "job results: $results"
          for r in $results; do
            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
              exit 1
            fi
          done
          echo "all required jobs succeeded"

    # #H-007: an UNKNOWN/empty-license package must not pass SILENTLY (it could hide a copyleft) —
    # it is surfaced as a ::warning::; the gate still only FAILS on explicit deny matches.
    import json

    gate = _load_license_gate()
    report = [
        {"Name": "mystery", "Version": "1", "License": "UNKNOWN"},
        {"Name": "blank", "Version": "1", "License": ""},
        {"Name": "ok", "Version": "1", "License": "MIT"},
    ]
    p = tmp_path / "licenses.json"
    p.write_text(json.dumps(report), encoding="utf-8")
    import io
    from contextlib import redirect_stdout

    buf = io.StringIO()
    with redirect_stdout(buf):
        rc = gate.main(str(p))
    out = buf.getvalue()
    assert rc == 0  # unknown licenses do not FAIL the gate (would be too brittle)
    assert "::warning::" in out and "mystery" in out and "blank" in out  # but are surfaced


def test_ci_has_least_privilege_permissions():
    # #H-008: the workflow must pin the GITHUB_TOKEN to read-only at the top level.
    text = _ci_text()
    assert "permissions:" in text and "contents: read" in text, "no least-privilege token (#H-008)"


def test_ci_integration_verifies_services_are_reachable():
    # #H-009: the integration job must fail loudly if the service containers aren't reachable, so
    # the live tests can't silently SKIP and let the gate pass vacuously.
    text = _ci_text()
    assert "service containers are reachable" in text or "never became ready" in text, (
        "integration job has no service-readiness gate (#H-009)"
    )
    assert "pg_isready" in text and "/readyz" in text

content.text:
File restored (clean git status, no diff). 

Let me confirm the YAML is valid and that the test discrimination is robust — note a subtle point: the test only checks for substring presence anywhere in the file. Even if `contents: read` were inside a job-level block, the test would pass. But the actual fix places it at top level, which is correct. Let me verify validity.

input.description: Validate YAML and check top-level permissions

content.text:
Verification complete.

Findings:
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` has a valid top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` block (lines 11-12), parsed by a real YAML loader. This is the idiomatic, least-privilege pin for `GITHUB_TOKEN`. All jobs (core, integration, audit, all-checks) only read code and upload artifacts; `upload-artifact` does not require write token perms, so read-only at top level is correct with no regression. The accompanying comment correctly notes any job needing more must opt in with its own `permissions:`.
- Test `tests/test_supply_chain.py::test_ci_has_least_privilege_permissions` (line 301) asserts both `"permissions:"` and `"contents: read"` appear in the file. It PASSES on the fixed file and FAILS when I reverted the top-level block (zero `contents: read` remained), so it discriminates.

Minor note (not a CONCERN): the test is substring-based, so it would also pass if `contents: read` were only in a job-level block rather than top level. But the actual fix is correct at top level, and reverting the real fix makes the test fail, satisfying discrimination. File restored to clean state after testing.

input.reasoning: Appropriate: .github/workflows/ci.yml now has a valid top-level `permissions: {contents: read}` block (lines 11-12), confirmed by a real yaml.safe_load. This is the idiomatic least-privilege pin for GITHUB_TOKEN. No regression: all jobs only checkout/read code and upload artifacts (upload-artifact needs no write token scope), so read-only top-level is sufficient; the comment correctly tells any job needing more to opt in with its own permissions block. Complete/discriminating: tests/test_supply_chain.py::test_ci_has_least_privilege_permissions (line 301) asserts both 'permissions:' and 'contents: read' are present. It PASSES on the fixed file; I reverted the top-level block and it FAILED (AssertionError: no least-privilege token #H-008), since zero 'contents: read' remained — so it fails if reverted. File restored to clean git state afterward. Minor (non-blocking) caveat: the test is plain substring matching, so it does not strictly verify the block is TOP-LEVEL vs job-level — but the actual fix is top-level and reverting it breaks the test, satisfying discrimination.

content.text: CONFIRMED. H-008 fix is both appropriate and complete. The top-level `permissions: {contents: read}` block is present and valid in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, and the test `test_ci_has_least_privilege_permissions` discriminates (fails when the fix is reverted).
